title: Bearing resistance of reinforced soil–aggregate systems
author_string: Nair, Asha M, Madhavi Latha, G.
journal: Proceedings of the Institution of Civil Engineers - Ground Improvement
abstract: This paper presents the results of laboratory California bearing ratio tests carried out on compacted unreinforced and reinforced soil–aggregate systems. The soil used in these tests is clay of low plasticity and the tests were conducted in the standard California bearing ratio mould. For reinforced soil–aggregate systems, layers of geosynthetics were introduced at the interface of the soil and aggregate layers and sometimes within the aggregate layer also. The various geosynthetics used in these tests were a geotextile, a biaxial geogrid and a geonet. The water content used during compaction was varied in different tests to study the effect of the water content and the unit weight of the soil on the bearing resistance of the reinforced soil–aggregates. The effect of the quantity of reinforcement and the form of reinforcement was also studied. As expected, the bearing resistance of soil–aggregate systems is improved with an increase in quantity or the stiffness of the reinforcement. It is observed that with an increase in water content, the reinforcement becomes more effective in improving the bearing resistance of the soil–aggregate systems. Furthermore, geosynthetics in the form of geocells are found to be more effective compared to planar layers because of the confinement effect.
